cataloging_subject.label: Sawles Warde
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous early-thirteenth-century Middle English prose homily Sawles Warde. The work is distinct from its three surviving manuscript witnesses, d'Ardenne's 1977 Bodley-based transcription, the 1989 OTA deposit, U-M's digital representation, and this repository's XML artifact. The checked-in representation also retains a Royal-witness scribal colophon and nine-line prayer after the homily; those witness-specific additions are separately scoped and do not make the abstract work mixed-form.

catalog_summary.title: Sawles Warde
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.editor: Simonne R. T. O. d'Ardenne (probable electronic source); the inherited U-M header instead names Richard Middlewood Wilson
catalog_summary.date: Early thirteenth century, approximately 1210; all three surviving witnesses are first-half-thirteenth-century copies
catalog_summary.region: West Midlands, England; Herefordshire is a qualified association rather than a secure composition site

assertion.notes: The title of J. R. R. Tolkien's modern-English translation, published in 2026; it is an alternate translated title, not the title of the U-M encoding.

notes: No named author is securely established. Similarity to other Katherine Group works does not prove common authorship.

scope.description: The Bodley-based 1977 transcription that textual and pagination evidence identifies as the probable print ancestor of the electronic stream

assertion.notes: This records responsibility for a probable source edition, not authorship of the medieval work; the exact CTILIB-to-print transfer remains undocumented.

scope.description: The 1938 three-manuscript edition named by the inherited U-M source description

assertion.notes: Wilson's editorial responsibility is certain, but the claim that his edition directly supplied the electronic text conflicts with the electronic pagination and d'Ardenne/Helsinki alignment.

Tolkien dated the work to about 1210; Wilson proposed about 1210–15 or slightly earlier from linguistic evidence and explicitly called the result approximate. No numeric interval is normalized from those qualitative estimates.

notes: The XML header's bare 1240 belongs to inherited witness-era cataloging and must not be treated as an exact composition year.

date_statements.display: Survives in three broadly first-half-thirteenth-century witnesses; their priority is unresolved

assertion.notes: Bodley 34, Royal 17 A XXVII, and Cotton Titus D XVIII are all broadly assigned to the first half of the thirteenth century; the evidence does not establish which is earliest. Individual copy dates remain on the lineage witness entities.

notes: No numeric interval is normalized from the shared qualitative dating.

assertion.notes: The broader Katherine/AB Group is associated with the Welsh Marches; this does not establish one exact composition site.

Wilson judged Herefordshire possible but not demonstrable; the current Bodleian catalog separately gives a low-certainty possible Hereford origin for Bodley 34. Witness provenance and scribal dialect are not converted into a secure work-composition claim.

scope.description: The biblical incipit and brief scriptural formulae embedded in the Middle English homily

form_statements.description: Rhythmic, densely alliterative prose. The medieval work and surviving witnesses present the homily as prose rather than verse. The nine-line rhymed prayer is a Royal-witness scribal addition and is separately scoped; it does not make the abstract homily mixed-form.
A nine-line rhymed prayer following the Royal scribal colophon; the repository XML flattens it into one P element.

subject_statements.label: Guardianship and salvation of the soul
Death, Hell, Heaven, fear, and divine love
Spiritual vigilance and defense against temptation
Female contemplative and anchoritic spiritual life

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship supports a female contemplative audience, but the exact institutional readership is not directly documented.

summaries.value: An anonymous early Middle English prose homily that imagines the body as a house guarding the soul. Reason governs the household; Will and the five senses require discipline; the four cardinal virtues defend the house; and messengers describing Hell and Heaven teach fear of damnation and love of eternal life. The work freely adapts the Latin De custodia interioris hominis into an expanded dramatic allegory.

The checked-in XML is one complete composite electronic reading, not three parallel manuscript transcriptions. It follows a Bodley-based edited stream through the damaged and lost end of Bodley 34, then preserves Royal readings and the Royal-only colophon and verse prayer. Its page milestones 165–186 align with d'Ardenne 1977, while its header instead claims Wilson 1938; that source conflict is retained in the paired lineage manifest.

extent.description: One prose homily followed by two Royal-witness additions. The checked-in XML BODY contains 1 HEAD, 29 P, 22 PB numbered 165–186, and no LG or L elements. P1–P27 encode the homily, P28 the Royal scribal colophon, and P29 a nine-line rhymed prayer flattened as prose markup. The XML omits d'Ardenne's opening Trinitarian invocation and the printed edition's front matter, back matter, and notes.

content_parts.label: Sawles Warde
content_parts.summary: The complete allegorical homily, beginning with Matthew 24:43 / Luke 12:39 and concluding with a Trinitarian prayer and Amen.
notes: Bodley 34 is defective at the end; the electronic stream is therefore not a literal transcription of one extant manuscript throughout.

content_parts.label: Royal scribal colophon
content_parts.summary: A witness-specific request to say a Paternoster for Johan, identified as the person who wrote the book.
notes: Johan is treated as the Royal copyist named by the colophon, not as author of the abstract homily.

content_parts.label: Royal nine-line prayer for the copyist
content_parts.summary: A nine-line rhymed prayer asking readers to pray for the first-person speaker's soul.

locators.value: BODY/P[29], nine verse lines flattened into one paragraph

related_works.title: De custodia interioris hominis
notes: A Latin dialogue or sermon associated with, but probably not authored by, Anselm of Canterbury. Sawles Warde freely adapts and expands it; the older attribution to Hugh of Saint-Victor's De anima is rejected by current scholarship. No exact physical Latin exemplar is claimed.

related_works.title: The Katherine Group
notes: Sawles Warde is one of five prose texts preserved together in Bodley 34 and conventionally grouped by manuscript context and AB language; the group label does not establish one author.

related_works.title: Ancrene Wisse
notes: The works share AB-language and female-contemplative contexts; no direct source relation or common authorship is asserted.

evidence.summary: Primary repository artifact; supplies the U-M/OTA identifiers, inherited Wilson 1938 source description, 1240 legacy date, page milestones 165–186, complete homily, Royal colophon, and Royal verse prayer.

evidence.citation: Element, boundary, and text-stream inspection of Sawleswd.xml, performed 2026-07-15.
evidence.summary: Found 1 HEAD, 29 P, 22 PB numbered 165–186, and no LG or L elements. P1–P27 comprise the homily, P28 the Royal colophon, and P29 a nine-line Royal prayer flattened as prose markup; normalized text was also compared with the OTA deposit and Helsinki sample.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan CME item Sawleswd, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Current item title, anonymous authorship, Wilson 1938 catalog claim, OTA number U-1323-A, persistent identifier, public access, and No Copyright–United States statement.

evidence.citation: Oxford Text Archive item 1323 and exact deposited files, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Confirms the 8 August 1989 John Cooper deposit, complete 25.3-KB text, depositor statement ‘as seen on CTILIB,’ OTA metadata history, and current CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 distribution.

evidence.citation: Helsinki Corpus Sawles Warde sample and exact TEI XML, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Explicitly identifies d'Ardenne 1977, pages 165.20–183.24, as source for a 3,831-word sample and supplies a text stream aligning with the OTA/U-M reading.

evidence.citation: Direct normalized comparison of repository, OTA, and Helsinki text streams, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: After legacy character-code normalization, OTA and repository alphabetic streams agree at 99.98 percent, differing in two short readings; the Helsinki d'Ardenne sample also aligns closely and shares the 165–183 pagination.

evidence.citation: R. M. Wilson's complete 1938 edition and derivatives, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Provides the inherited header's named edition, three-witness diplomatic text, reconstructed stemma, approximate 1210–15 date, West Midlands/possible Herefordshire localization, prose-form argument, and Royal-only ending.

evidence.citation: Bodleian catalog record and official cached TEI for MS Bodley 34, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Confirms Sawles Warde at ff. 72r–80v, two missing leaves after fol. 80, Middle English AB language, first-half-thirteenth-century South-West Midlands origin, and low-certainty possible Hereford association.

evidence.citation: British Library records for Royal MS 17 A XXVII, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Confirms Sawles Warde at ff. 1r–10v, first-half-thirteenth-century date, Middle English/West Midlands context, Johan colophon and nine-line prayer, and current physical-access conditions.

evidence.citation: British Library records for Cotton MS Titus D XVIII, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Confirms Sawles Warde at ff. 105v–112v within a first-half-thirteenth-century Middle English component and records that the legacy digital images are currently unavailable.

evidence.citation: Huber and Robertson's complete 2016 Katherine Group edition, documented and cached in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Modern Bodley-based edition, translation, and notes establishing the work's allegorical homiletic contents, prose form, corrected De custodia source, Bodley/Royal transition, Royal-only ending, audience context, and witness handling.

evidence.citation: Cossio and Goering's 2026 critical edition and study of Tolkien's Soul's Ward, documented in the linked lineage manifest.
evidence.summary: Current open-access scholarship for the early-thirteenth-century prose homily, approximate 1210 Tolkien dating, West Midlands AB language, adaptation of De custodia, three exact witness loci, female religious audience, and d'Ardenne's Bodley-centered editorial procedure.

open_questions.question: What exact CTILIB object and print source produced the 1989 OTA deposit, and when were d'Ardenne's page milestones added?
next_steps: Locate surviving CTILIB documentation or backups and identify the exact display seen by John Cooper on 8 August 1989.
Compare a physical copy of d'Ardenne 1977, pages 165–186, character by character with the OTA deposit and U-M page boundaries.
Ask OTA and U-M whether migration or conversion logs survive for item 1323 / U-1323-A.
notes: The current evidence strongly favors d'Ardenne 1977 over Wilson 1938 as the print ancestor, but does not document the complete transfer chain.

open_questions.question: Can Sawles Warde's composition site and audience be narrowed beyond a West Midlands work for a female contemplative or cloistered readership?
next_steps: Review current AB-language localization work separately for composition, archetype, and each surviving witness.
Keep Herefordshire, anchoritic, and cloistered-nun claims qualified unless direct evidence narrows them.

open_questions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of De custodia interioris hominis underlies Sawles Warde?
next_steps: Consult Becker's source studies and current work on the pseudo-Anselmian De moribus tradition.
Do not substitute the obsolete Hugh of Saint-Victor / De anima attribution or claim an extant physical exemplar without textual evidence.

notes: The XML's page range 165–186 and text stream are evidence about an editorial/digital carrier, not manuscript foliation and not a medieval composition date.
Johan's colophon and prayer belong specifically to Royal MS 17 A XXVII; they are retained by the electronic stream but not generalized into abstract-work authorship or mixed form.
Rights, access, source conflicts, reconstructed lost exemplars, and complete-object facsimile findings are recorded in the paired lineage manifest.
